title: Willow laundry basket
subtitle: Oval willow laundry basket in the English country tradition
excerpt: A large oval willow laundry basket: 55 cm long, 40 cm wide, 35 cm deep, with a fixed base and hinged lid. The basket uses standard randing weave on an oval base staked-and-tied at the join. This is an advanced project requiring 2 to 3 days of steady weaving; the finished basket will last for decades.
sourceNotes: Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for household basket forms; Victorian basketry manuals including the sections of Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics (1885) on basket work.

glossaryTerms.definition: Willow basketry weave in which a single rod is woven over and under alternate stakes, advancing one stake per stroke.
A tight basketry weave using three or more rods worked together that strengthens the base-to-side transition and the rim.
The step in basket-making in which the upright stakes are inserted alongside the base spokes and bent up to form the sides of the basket.
The first few rows of waling worked immediately after staking-up; the upsett locks the stakes into their upright position and sets the basket's footprint.
The crossing of base sticks at the start of an oval or round willow basket base. The slath sets the base dimensions.

content.text: A laundry basket is the largest domestic willow basket: an oval form, deep enough to hold a full household wash load, with a lid that closes against moisture. The construction is classic English country: oval
, and a separate lid worked on the same base dimensions. This is a substantial three-day project for a confident intermediate basketmaker; not a first basket.

Brown willow (dried and re-soaked) or buff willow (boiled and stripped). For a 55 cm basket at this scale: approximately 4 kg of willow, mainly 4 to 5 foot lengths for the stakes and 3 foot lengths for the weavers. Brown willow needs soaking for 1 week per year of dry storage before it is pliable. Work with the willow immediately after soaking; set it in a damp cloth if the session runs over 2 hours.

Build the oval base from heavy base sticks: 6 sticks 55 cm long for the length stakes, 4 sticks 40 cm long for the width. Cut slots through the three central length sticks and thread the four width sticks through to form the slath. Tie off the slath with two lengths of fine willow and work 4 to 5 rows of pairing weave to fill the base, spacing the spokes evenly at 5 cm intervals at the rim. The finished base should measure 55 cm by 40 cm.

Use a bodkin to insert two stakes alongside each base spoke, bending them upright at the base edge. Work the upsett immediately: 3 rows of three-rod waling. The upsett is what holds the basket's shape; loose upsett waling will allow the sides to lean outward. Rap each row down firmly with the rapping iron.

Work the main body in randing to the full 35 cm depth. Join new rods by inserting the new tip alongside the old tip and working both for 3 stakes before dropping the old. Rap every 4 to 5 rows. Check the basket outline regularly against a straightedge; the oval must not become round.

Finish the body with 3 rows of three-rod waling, then work a 4-rod border. The lid is a separate oval basket without sides, worked to the same 55 by 40 cm base dimensions, then fitted with leather hinges at the back and rope handles at the sides.
Open-top version: the lid is omitted and replaced with a rope handle at each end.
Smaller linen basket: 45 cm long, 30 cm wide, 25 cm deep; one day of weaving for an experienced maker.

Wipe down with a damp cloth once a year to prevent the willow becoming brittle. If any stakes or weavers crack and poke through, push them back with the bodkin and tuck the end behind the nearest weaver. A well-made willow laundry basket lasts 20 to 30 years with ordinary use.

items.symptom: Sides lean outward as height increases
items.cause: Upsett waling too loose; or stakes not held upright during randing
items.fix: Re-work the upsett more tightly on the next basket. During randing, hold the stakes vertical with one hand and weave with the other; do not allow the weaving pressure to push the stakes outward.

items.symptom: Oval base becomes round during randing
items.cause: Even number of stakes on both axes; or uneven tension between the long and short sides
items.fix: Use an odd total stake count (the standard count for oval randing). Deliberately keep a slightly higher tension on the long-axis rods than the short-axis rods.
